q: What are the two types of online portals for alternative finance?

options.A: Debt portals and equity portals, split by the security type issued.
options.B: P2P portals and marketplace portals, split by who provides funding.
options.C: Investment and non-investment portals, split by what contributors expect in return.
options.D: Balance sheet portals and donation portals, split by who holds the loan.

q: What are the two types of investment crowdfunding and how are they different?

options.A: P2P and balance sheet lending; one is an intermediary, the other a principal.
options.B: Debt and equity; debt is a loan repaid with interest, equity is an ownership stake.
options.C: Donation-based and rewards-based; one promises a reward, the other nothing.
options.D: All-or-nothing and keep-it-all; they differ on when funds are collected.

q: What are the key differences between rewards-based and donation-based crowdfunding?

options.A: Rewards-based pre-sells a future product for a reward; donation-based promises no benefit.
options.B: Rewards-based issues equity stakes; donation-based issues repayable loans.
options.C: Rewards-based funds charities and disaster relief; donation-based funds new products.
options.D: Rewards-based is all-or-nothing; donation-based is always keep-it-all.

q: Why are peer-to-peer (P2P) platforms now called marketplace platforms?

options.A: Because platforms now hold the loans on their own balance sheets like banks.
options.B: Because regulators required the name change under the 1933 Securities Act.
options.C: Because they now sell equity stakes alongside debt to the crowd.
options.D: Because most funding now comes from institutional investors, not individuals.

q: What is a balance sheet lender and why are the incentives different from a P2P/marketplace lender?

options.A: It is an intermediary matching borrowers with lenders; it passes default risk to lenders.
options.B: It acts like a bank, funding and holding loans itself, so it bears default risk.
options.C: It only serves accredited investors, so it faces less regulatory oversight.
options.D: It earns underwriting commissions on volume, creating misaligned incentives with lenders.

q: Who is borrowing from online lenders and who is providing funding? How do lenders assess the risk of default for online loans?

options.A: Only large corporations borrow; only retail investors fund; risk is assessed by collateral value.
options.B: Individuals and businesses borrow; only banks fund; risk is set by a fixed 30% APR cap.
options.C: Individuals and small businesses borrow; retail and institutional investors fund; portals assign credit ratings priced into interest rates.
options.D: Governments borrow; institutional investors fund; risk is assessed solely by FICO score thresholds.

q: How is crowdfunding regulated and what are the risks to individual investors or lenders?

options.A: Regulation varies by country with no global standard; key risks are credit, illiquidity, poor disclosure, and fraud.
options.B: A single global regulator oversees all portals; the only real risk is illiquidity.
options.C: Portals are unregulated everywhere; investors face credit risk but are protected against fraud.
options.D: The SEC regulates all portals worldwide; losses always fall on the platform, not investors.

q: How successful have crowdfunding platforms been in disrupting traditional financial intermediaries such as banks?

options.A: Highly successful; portals replaced banks and their share prices outperformed the market.
options.B: Successful on liquidity and trust, but they failed to reduce information asymmetry.
options.C: They fully disrupted lending, forcing banks like WebBank to exit consumer credit.
options.D: Rather than disrupting, they became an alternative digital channel, with many pioneers forced to pivot after losses.

q: What has been the growth of alternative finance globally and where is the most capital raised? What has happened in China?

options.A: It shrank to under $100 billion total; Europe leads; China still dominates at 86% share.
options.B: It raised nearly $1.5 trillion over eight years at 33% CAGR, now led by the US, after China collapsed amid fraud.
options.C: It grew steadily led by China, whose regulated P2P market remains the world's largest.
options.D: It stagnated with no real growth; the US and China split the market evenly with no fraud.

q: Has crowdfunding increased access to capital for underbanked communities? Are there biases based on gender, race, or other factors?

options.A: Yes, it fully closed the inclusion gap, and research finds no biases by gender or race.
options.B: No, it reduced credit access everywhere, and only gender bias against women exists.
options.C: Evidence on inclusion is mixed, and research finds racial biases in pricing and funding, with more nuanced gender findings.
options.D: Yes, it eliminated bank monopolies, though African American borrowers received lower mortgage rates.
